parentQuestion: Double vision looking down, head tilt compensation

scaffolds.q: Double vision (diplopia) means seeing two images of one object.
scaffolds.explain: When eyes do not align properly, each eye sees differently, creating double images.

scaffolds.q: Different muscles move your eye in different directions.
scaffolds.explain: Six muscles per eye work together. Each has a specific direction it pulls.

scaffolds.q: Looking down is needed for reading and walking down stairs.
scaffolds.explain: These activities require your eyes to look downward - a specific eye movement.

scaffolds.q: The trochlear nerve (CN IV) controls one muscle that pulls the eye down.
scaffolds.explain: CN IV controls only the superior oblique muscle, which helps look down and inward.

scaffolds.q: People often tilt their head to compensate for eye muscle problems.
scaffolds.explain: Tilting the head reduces the work the damaged muscle needs to do.

scaffolds.q: CN IV is the smallest cranial nerve and only controls one muscle.
scaffolds.explain: It is tiny but important - dedicated to just the superior oblique muscle.

scaffolds.q: Difficulty looking down and in, with head tilt, suggests CN IV damage.
scaffolds.explain: This specific pattern is the classic sign of trochlear nerve palsy.

scaffolds.q: CN IV controls the superior oblique muscle for downward gaze.
scaffolds.explain: The superior oblique depresses and intorts the eye - essential for looking down.

scaffolds.q: Head trauma can damage CN IV due to its long path in the skull.
scaffolds.explain: CN IV has the longest intracranial course, making it vulnerable to shearing forces.
